summary: s_in_progress set after xQueueSend — if ota_task completes between send and flag write, future OTAs permanently locked out
failure_scenario: ota_request() sets s_in_progress = true (line 175) AFTER xQueueSend() succeeds (line 170). The commit pins ota_task to Core 0 (priority 2) while the MQTT task runs at priority 5 on the same core — normally preventing preemption. However, if a priority-6 task (e.g., WiFi MAC) preempts the MQTT task between lines 170-175 and then blocks, the scheduler may run ota_task. If ota_task fully processes the URL (download + flash, potentially ~30s) and resets s_in_progress = false before line 175 executes, the subsequent write sets s_in_progress = true permanently. No URL remains in the queue (ota_task consumed it), so the next ota_request checks s_in_progress, sees true, and rejects. Device becomes deaf to OTA commands until reboot. Fix: move s_in_progress = true to BEFORE xQueueSend, with s_in_progress = false rollback if xQueueSend fails.

summary: cJSON_PrintUnformatted return value not checked for NULL before use as string argument
failure_scenario: handle_get_config() calls cJSON_PrintUnformatted(root) at line 65 and immediately uses json_str in strlen(json_str) (line 67) and httpd_resp_send(req, json_str, ...) without a NULL check. On ESP32-S3 with PSRAM (CONFIG_SPIRAM_USE_MALLOC=y), heap exhaustion or PSRAM fragmentation can cause cJSON_PrintUnformatted to return NULL. Passing NULL to strlen is undefined behavior — the ESP32 crashes with a data abort exception. The developer was aware of this pattern: data_pipeline.c:pipeline_build_report_json (line 113-117) has the correct NULL guard. Contrast with web_server.c which lacks it. Root cause: the diff changed free(json_str) to cJSON_free(json_str) but missed adding the NULL check the same function needs.

summary: handle_post_save numeric branch silently drops string-field values that parse as integers
failure_scenario: The POST handler checks if a form value parses as an integer via strtol (line 101). If so, it calls only the three numeric setters: nvs_config_set_u32 (line 105), nvs_config_set_u16 (line 106), nvs_config_set_u8 (line 107). The string setter (line 109 via else branch) is never called. None of the numeric setters recognize string-field keys like 'dev_name', 'mqtt_uri', 'mqtt_pass', or the new 'mqtt_client_id' — they all return ESP_ERR_NOT_FOUND silently. Impact: Setting mqtt_client_id=123 or dev_name=0 or wifi_ssid=9999 via the web form silently fails. The value is never stored in the RAM cache, and nvs_config_save() writes the stale previous value to NVS. The web form appears to accept the change (it reflects the POST value), but after reboot the old value returns. Fix: check the return value of all three numeric setters; if none matched, fall through to the string setter.

summary: No percent-decoding in handle_post_save — URL-encoded characters stored literally in NVS
failure_scenario: handle_post_save only replaces '+' with space (line 97) but never decodes percent-encoded sequences (%XX). The browser sends application/x-www-form-urlencoded data where %26 = &, %3D = =, %20 = space, %25 = %. These arrive at the ESP as literal %26/%3D sequences. For example, a client_id of 'foo&bar' is sent as 'foo%26bar', stored as 'foo%26bar', and the MQTT library uses the literal string 'foo%26bar' to connect — failing authentication because the broker expects 'foo&bar'. Affects all string fields including the new mqtt_client_id (which may need special chars depending on broker auth requirements). Fix: add a percent-decode loop after the plus-to-space substitution.

summary: strncpy without explicit null termination for new mqtt_client_id field — fragile reliance on prior struct zeroing
failure_scenario: strncpy(g_config.mqtt_client_id, value, sizeof(g_config.mqtt_client_id) - 1) does not null-terminate when strlen(value) >= sizeof(field) - 1 (i.e., 63 chars). Currently safe because nvs_config_init() memsets the entire g_config to zero before any strncpy call. But this fragile invariant is not enforced: if a future code path calls nvs_config_set_string on a non-zeroed buffer (e.g., a stack copy of config_t, or a reinitialization that skips memset), the mqtt_client_id[63] byte retains its prior value. Subsequent strlen(mqtt_client_id) reads into adjacent memory (mqtt_data_t field), returning an incorrect length. Any MQTT broker interaction with a corrupt client_id may cause authentication failures or connection drops. Fix: add explicit null termination after each strncpy: dst[sizeof(dst) - 1] = '\0';

summary: factory_reset_task uses signed int hold counter — undefined behavior on wraparound from sustained button press
failure_scenario: hold is int32_t, incremented every 100ms when button GPIO 9 is held. After 2,147,483,647 increments (~6.8 years of continuous press), signed overflow triggers C undefined behavior. The compiler may optimize away the while(1) loop, remove the hold % 10 == 0 check (line 122), or eliminate the hold >= RST_HOLD_THRESHOLD comparison (line 116) because a signed value can never exceed a positive threshold once it wraps negative. RST_HOLD_THRESHOLD=50 triggers esp_restart() after 5 seconds in practice, so overflow is never reached in normal use — but the latent UB means the compiler could legally delete the factory-reset logic entirely if it proves hold always wraps. Fix: change int hold to unsigned int or uint32_t.
note: Pre-existing bug, not introduced by this diff, but the app_main.c file was substantially restructured (wifi_manager_init moved, chip_info block relocated) making this an adjacent finding

summary: Partial init failure in modbus_uart_init leaks UART driver state — retry loop may become infinite
failure_scenario: If uart_param_config and uart_set_pin succeed but uart_driver_install fails (line 56-58), the function returns an error WITHOUT calling uart_driver_delete. s_uart_installed is NOT set (remains false from init). On retry (modbus_poll_task retry loop at app_main.c:233-236 with 5s delay), modbus_uart_deinit is called first by modbus_poll_task (line 232) — but it returns immediately because s_uart_installed is false. The retry then calls modbus_uart_init again: uart_param_config and uart_set_pin succeed again (ESP-IDF allows reconfiguration without deinit), but uart_driver_install returns ESP_ERR_INVALID_STATE because the driver is already installed from the first attempt. The retry loop spins forever: the port number (UART_NUM_1) is permanently held by the previous partial-driver allocation with no way to release it without a cold reboot. Fix: if uart_driver_install fails after a previous successful call (not the first attempt), call uart_driver_delete before returning error.

summary: saveptr declared uninitialized on first strtok_r call — works by POSIX spec but relies on implementation behavior
failure_scenario: char *saveptr is stack-declared without initialization (line 86) before the first strtok_r(buf, "&", &saveptr) call (line 87). On the first call, POSIX strtok_r passes buf to strtok internals and uses saveptr as an output parameter — it does not read the initial value. However, if a non-conforming libc implementation or a compiler sanitizer instruments saveptr as 'uninitialized', this could produce a runtime trap or false-positive diagnostic. Code audit tools (e.g., GCC -Wmaybe-uninitialized with LTO enabled on ESP-IDF) may flag this as a warning. Fix: initialize saveptr = NULL; on declaration.
note: Low severity — functionally correct with all POSIX and BSD strtok_r implementations, but triggers static-analysis warnings
